nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on clear-water outer reef slopes and offshore reefs, typically at depths of about 15–200 m though the species' overall depth range extends from roughly 3 m to over 250 m; reported to favour islands and offshore reefs over continental shelves and is absent from the Persian Gulf and Hawaii.

diet.notes: Generally best fed a varied carnivore diet of meaty marine foods including krill, raw table shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish; mysis and other frozen offerings have been reported to be readily accepted by acclimated specimens. Live feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food due to nutritional inadequacy and disease-transmission risk. Has been reported to feed once or twice daily on substantial meals appropriate to its large mouth and stomach capacity.

goodWith: large predatory fish of comparable size

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 4061x3046. Striking deep-red/maroon adult with purple-blue spots, yellow-edged fins and the diagnostic lyre-shaped tail; sharp, whole animal, vibrant full colour.
Totti; 3224x1802. Orange-red adult with pink-purple spots, yellow fin margins and lyretail clearly shown; crisp aquarium shot, whole fish in frame.
Frederic Ducarme; 2416x1815. In-situ reef shot of the darker blue-spotted colour phase, yellow-edged lyretail and red eye visible; adds natural-habitat variety.

summary: A large Indo-Pacific predatory grouper with a distinctive lyre-shaped caudal fin and yellow fin margins; generally considered an advanced-care, ambush predator that requires a very large display and tankmates of comparable size and disposition.
careNotes:
The Red Louti Grouper (Variola louti), also widely sold as the Lyretail Grouper, Yellow-edged Lyretail, Skunk Grouper, or Coronation Trout, is one of the larger members of the family Epinephelidae and among the most demanding groupers regularly offered to home aquarists. Wild adults have been documented to reach about 83 cm in total length, with a common adult size in the 70–75 cm range; juveniles imported at a few centimetres grow steadily and can outgrow under-sized displays within a few years.

A minimum tank of around 1,000 litres is generally advised, reflecting the species' adult size, heavy carnivorous waste output, and need for both ambush hiding spots and generous open swimming space. Aquarists who acquire small juveniles often start them in a smaller grow-out system and upgrade as the fish matures. Temperature recommendations across hobby and wild sources span a fairly wide range — wild occurrence records document 20–29°C — and a practical target of 22–27°C is broadly consistent with that span.

Wild-caught juveniles are typical in the trade and look strikingly different from adults, showing an orange to reddish-brown body with prominent white-edged spots. This juvenile colour phase has historically caused them to be mistaken for a separate species. Adults develop the species' characteristic red body with yellow-edged fin margins and the lyre-shaped tail that gives rise to the 'Lyretail' common name.

The Red Louti Grouper is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male once it reaches sufficient size. This is a trait shared across much of the grouper family.

Reef compatibility is generally regarded as requiring caution: the species poses essentially no threat to corals but has been reported to consume any fish or motile invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp, small fish, and crustaceans. It is not known to possess venomous spines, is not strictly nocturnal, and is not considered a significant escape risk in a well-covered marine system. The species tends to be relatively hardy and disease-resistant once acclimated, though it can be sensitive to water-quality swings given the heavy biological load a large predator's diet produces.

The advanced care rating reflects the long-term housing demands — 1,000 litres or more — the husbandry commitment required to maintain water quality for a large messy predator, and the risk of predation on tankmates, rather than any particular fragility of the fish itself.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Red Louti Grouper has been reported only very rarely in hobby literature and is not considered an established practice. As a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning individuals begin life as female — the dominant fish in a group can transition to male upon reaching sufficient size, with wild sexual maturity reported at around 41–48 cm. Wild reproduction takes the form of pelagic spawning, where eggs and larvae are released into the open water column above reef habitat.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page used for hobby care values: 300 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 30 in adult size, Moderate care, Aggressive temperament, Reef Safe with Caution, Carnivore.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Originally assigned secondary; HTTP 403 on access. Cross-verification done via the remaining slice secondaries instead.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae, Perciformes), maximum size (83 cm TL, common 75 cm), lifespan (7–15 yr), depth range (3–300 m, typically below 15 m), wild temperature (20.1–28.9°C, mean 27.2°C), Indo-Pacific distribution and absence from the Persian Gulf and Hawaii, and trophic level (~4.3, fish + crustacean predator).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium care values: 500 L minimum tank, 22–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.026, ~70 cm adult, Moderate to difficult care, semi-aggressive/territorial, carnivore, confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism, captive breeding rare. Tank size notably smaller than LiveAquaria; the value used (1000 L) was rounded toward the cautious-larger side.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (32 in / ~81 cm), 200 gal minimum tank, temperament (aggressive ambush predator), reef-safe with caution, carnivore diet (krill/shrimp/squid/clam/mussel). BlueZoo's 'Beginner' care-level rating is an outlier vs LiveAquaria and Reeflings; not used.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been meaningfully established in the hobby. In the wild, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — dominant females transition to male upon reaching sufficient size, with maturity reported at around 41–48 cm.
